ontology.foodex2_gap_note: No FoodEx2 code exists specifically for bubble tea with milk and no topping; A0FKP (tea-based beverages with milk) is the closest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for bubble tea with milk; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of bubble tea with milk and no topping is approximately 500 ml (500 g), as typically sold at Singaporean bubble tea stalls and chains.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble Tea with Milk (No Topping) — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Bubble Tea with Milk (No Topping), cr_id D08010010, confidence 0.95. Saturated fat estimated from USDA FoodData Central entry for milk tea (USDA SR Legacy #14355) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; see notes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sugar-sweetened milk tea beverages — analogous estimate based on sweetened milk tea GI data
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI entry exists for Singaporean bubble tea with milk and no topping in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 70 (high) is estimated from the closest analogous entry: sweetened milk tea / tea with sugar and milk, which consistently returns GI values of 65–75 in the Sydney database. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (70 × 30.7) / 100 ≈ 21.5, based on a 500 g serving containing 6.14 g carbohydrate per 100 g (30.7 g per 500 g serving). Input record GI confidence was 0.455, reflecting the analogous nature of the estimate.

hypertension.key_factor: Sugar-sweetened beverage (6.14 g sugar/100 g) exceeding the 5 g/100 g SSB threshold; independent blood pressure risk regardless of low sodium (12 mg/100 g)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid standard-sugar bubble tea, as its sugar-sweetened beverage status independently elevates blood pressure risk per AHA 2018 guidelines; request unsweetened or zero-sugar versions only.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High GI (70), 6.14 g sugar/100 g with zero dietary fibre, and potential trans fat from non-dairy creamer worsening insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid this beverage due to its high glycaemic index, liquid sugar content (6.14 g/100 g), and absence of fibre, all of which cause rapid postprandial glucose excursions; if consumed, request unsweetened or zero-sugar formulation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Estimated trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) from non-dairy creamer, saturated fat (~1.5 g/100 g), and sugar-sweetened beverage status contributing to dyslipidaemia and endothelial dysfunction
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this beverage due to likely trans fat content from non-dairy creamer and its sugar-sweetened beverage classification; choose unsweetened tea with fresh low-fat milk as a safer alternative.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bubble Tea with Milk (No Topping) is a freshly prepared sweetened milk tea beverage popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia, sold widely at bubble tea chains and hawker stalls. It consists of brewed black or green tea blended with non-dairy creamer or fresh milk and sugar syrup, served chilled over ice, without any additional toppings such as tapioca pearls, grass jelly, or pudding. The drink has a smooth, milky, lightly sweet flavour with a subtle tea bitterness. Per 100 g it provides 46 kcal, 0.32 g protein, 2.41 g fat, and 6.14 g carbohydrate (all as sugar), with zero dietary fibre and low sodium (12 mg). The fat content is primarily from non-dairy creamer, which commonly contains palm oil and may contain partially hydrogenated oils contributing trace trans fat (~0.1 g/100 g). The glycaemic index is estimated at 70 (high), yielding a glycaemic load of approximately 21.5 per standard 500 ml serving. Clinically, this beverage is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) and is unsuitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease due to its high sugar content, high GI, absence of fibre, and potential trans fat from non-dairy creamer. Consumers seeking a healthier option should request unsweetened or zero-sugar formulations and specify fresh dairy milk over non-dairy creamer.
common_user_queries: How many calories in milk tea?
Is milk tea high in sugar?
Is bubble tea suitable for hypertension?
What is the GI of bubble tea?
Does bubble tea contain trans fat?
Is boba tea good for weight loss?

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id D08010010, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singaporean food items. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy closest analogue. GI estimated from University of Sydney GI Database analogous entry for sweetened milk tea (confidence medium for GI component). Overall record confidence is high given exact HPB source match for core nutrients.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (1.5 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry #14355 (tea, prepared, with whole milk) as HPB does not separately report saturated fat for this item; this is an adapted estimate and should be updated when HPB publishes detailed fatty acid data. 2. Trans fat (0.1 g/100 g) is estimated based on the known composition of non-dairy creamers commonly used in Singaporean bubble tea; this value should be verified against specific product labels. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1 as detailed micronutrient and fatty acid data for this specific bubble tea formulation are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026. 4. dietary_fibre_g is confirmed zero per HPB source data — this beverage contains no fibre-contributing ingredients. 5. GI of 70 is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database based on sweetened milk tea entries; the input record GI confidence of 0.455 reflects this uncertainty. 6. Glycaemic load per serving calculated as (70 × 30.7 g carbohydrate per 500 g serving) / 100 = 21.5. 7. The food is classified as a sugar-sweetened beverage (SSB) per both the diabetes and hypertension hard gates (sugar ≥ 5 g/100 g in a beverage with zero fibre). 8. Halal status is assumed based on typical Singaporean bubble tea formulations; consumers should verify non-dairy creamer ingredients for specific brands.
